mission.title: Install and Configure Redis Operator (Spotahome / OT-CONTAINER-KIT)
mission.description: The Redis Operator by OT-CONTAINER-KIT simplifies the deployment and management of Redis instances on Kubernetes, supporting standalone, cluster, and sentinel modes. It is ideal for users looking to automate Redis setup with built-in monitoring and best practices. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Helm repository for the Redis Operator, allowing you to install it using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add redis-operator https://ot-container-kit.github.io/redis-operator

```

> This command adds the repository to your Helm configuration.

steps.title: Install Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for the operator to manage Redis resources. This step installs them from the Helm chart.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OT-CONTAINER-KIT/redis-operator/v0.24.0/deploy/crds/redis.redis.opstree.dev_crd.yaml

```

> This command installs the required CRDs for the Redis Operator.

steps.title: Install Redis Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the Redis Operator using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

helm install redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --create-namespace --version 0.24.0

```

> This command deploys the Redis Operator in the 'redis-operator' namespace.

steps.title: Create a Redis Cluster
steps.description:
This step creates a Redis cluster using a custom resource definition (CR) to define the cluster configuration.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: Redis
metadata:
  name: my-redis
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  mode: cluster
  replicas: 3
EOF

```

> This command creates a Redis cluster named 'my-redis' with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: Redis
metadata:
  name: my-redis
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  mode: cluster
  replicas: 3
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Redis Cluster
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Redis cluster to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get redis -n redis-operator

```

> This command lists the Redis resources in the 'redis-operator' namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Redis Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Redis Operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all Redis instances managed by the operator, leading to potential data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall redis-operator --namespace redis-operator

kubectl delete namespace redis-operator

```

> This command uninstalls the Redis Operator from the specified namespace.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Redis Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your Redis data. This step includes draining the operator, upgrading it, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl drain <operator-pod-name> --ignore-daemonsets

helm upgrade redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --version 0.24.0

kubectl get pods -n redis-operator

```

> Replace <operator-pod-name> with the actual pod name of the Redis Operator.
> This command upgrades the Redis Operator to the specified version.
> This command checks the status of the pods to ensure they are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create Redis instance'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to insufficient permissions or misconfigured CRDs.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the CRDs are correctly applied and that the operator has the necessary permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Redis cluster is not healthy'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if one or more Redis pods are not running or are in a crash loop.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Redis pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to Redis'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate network issues or misconfigured service endpoints.

**Fix:**
Verify the service configuration and ensure that network policies allow traffic to the Redis pods.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to schedule pod'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources available to schedule the Redis pods.

**Fix:**
Scale up your Kubernetes cluster or adjust the resource requests/limits in the Redis CR.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Redis 7.x, improved monitoring capabilities with Grafana dashboards.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.19, ensure to update your Redis CR configurations to match new specifications.

resolution.summary: You should see the Redis cluster listed with 'kubectl get redis -n redis-operator', showing the status as 'Running'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: Redis
metadata:
  name: my-redis
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  mode: cluster
  replicas: 3
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources and the required tools installed.
